questions.question: Why is one global rate limit usually insufficient for abuse prevention?
options: Because all attackers use the same API key.
Because abuse patterns differ by path, identity, cost, and attack style, so one dimension misses too much.
Because edge systems cannot count requests.
Because rate limiting only works for internal traffic.
questions.explanation: Abuse control usually needs multiple layers and key dimensions, not one blunt limiter.

questions.question: What is the goal of adding friction such as challenges?
options: To make every request slower so the backend does less work.
To increase cost for suspicious automation without blocking all uncertain traffic immediately.
To replace authentication entirely.
To eliminate the need for observability.
questions.explanation: Friction helps separate suspicious automation from healthy humans when confidence is not absolute.

questions.question: Why is IP-only abuse enforcement risky for login protection?
options: Because every user has a unique IP address.
Because shared NATs and rotating attacker IPs make IP alone both unfair and weak.
Because login systems cannot read IP metadata.
Because IPs only matter in single-region networks.
questions.explanation: Healthy users may share IPs, while attackers often rotate them.

questions.question: Which traffic should usually be protected with the earliest and cheapest controls?
options: Traffic that is obviously bad or untrusted before origin work is spent on it.
Only traffic from premium customers.
Only responses larger than 1 MB.
Only requests that already passed all expensive business logic.
questions.explanation: Early cheap enforcement preserves origin capacity and lowers attack amplification.

questions.question: What metric most directly shows collateral damage from an abuse rule?
options: High deny or challenge rate for healthy enterprise traffic with support complaints.
Average CPU temperature on developer laptops.
Only the total number of requests.
Number of markdown files in the repository.
questions.explanation: Collateral damage appears as false positives on legitimate traffic and customer pain.

questions.question: Why add tenant-aware budgets on expensive APIs?
options: To protect shared backend cost and fairness even when one customer is not technically abusive.
To replace route-level monitoring.
To disable all enterprise traffic during bursts.
To avoid tracking account identity.
questions.explanation: Tenant-aware controls limit noisy-neighbor harm and backend cost blowups.

questions.question: When is fail-closed behavior most justified in an abuse layer?
options: On low-risk content reads where user friction matters most.
On high-risk flows like credential attacks where allowing abuse is more damaging than short-term friction.
Whenever an observability dashboard is slow.
Never, because all abuse systems should fail open.
questions.explanation: Credential attack paths often justify stricter degraded-mode behavior.

questions.question: What is the strongest reason to keep block explanations?
options: They help debug false positives, tune policies, and defend decisions to customers.
They remove the need for rate limiting storage.
They guarantee lower p99 latency.
They allow challenges to be skipped forever.
questions.explanation: Explainability is critical for operations and trust when policies affect legitimate users.
